_comment: Type C — Chain Conditional / Affirming the Consequent (Q3-style / theft investigation). Fallacy: affirming_the_consequent. High confidence.
source_text: Whoever stole the money from the school office must have had both an opportunity to access the office and a motive for taking the money. Finn had access to the office that afternoon, and he had financial difficulties at the time. Therefore Finn must have stolen the money.

premises.text: Whoever stole the money must have had both an opportunity to access the office AND a motive. (Stole → Opportunity ∧ Motive)
Finn had access to the office that afternoon (Opportunity).
Finn had financial difficulties at the time (Motive).

conclusion.text: Finn must have stolen the money.

fallacies.explanation: P1 states that stealing is a sufficient condition for having opportunity and motive (Stole → Opp ∧ Motive). P2 and P3 establish that Finn has opportunity and motive. The conclusion infers that Finn stole (Opp ∧ Motive → Stole). This reverses the conditional — affirming the consequent — which is invalid. Many people could have opportunity and motive without being the thief.
